name: pyupgrade (Automatically Upgrade Python Syntax)

performance.notes: Fast tokenizer-based rewriting; processes thousands of files in seconds

agent_readiness.notes: Automatically upgrades old Python syntax to modern idioms. pyupgrade --py39-plus *.py. pyupgrade --py311-plus src/. Fixes: % formatting → f-strings, .format() → f-strings, old-style super() → super(), dict() → {}, set() → {}, typing.List → list (3.9+), typing.Optional → X | None (3.10+), typing.Union → X | Y (3.10+), open(f, 'r') → open(f). Pre-commit: pre-commit hook. --py36-plus through --py313-plus flags. Works with ruff (which now includes many same fixes). Idempotent rewrites.

gotchas: Must specify minimum Python version with --py3X-plus
Some upgrades are irreversible (e.g., typing.Optional → X | None requires Python 3.10+)
ruff --fix now handles many pyupgrade rules — may overlap
Rarely breaks code but some edge cases with string formatting exist
best_when: Migrating legacy Python 2/3 codebases to modern syntax
Enforcing modern type annotation style (PEP 604, PEP 585)
Pre-commit hook for automatic syntax modernization
avoid_when: Projects that must support old Python versions the upgrade targets
When ruff already handles the needed rules (redundant)
Code that must be readable by users on older Python versions
summary: pyupgrade automatically upgrades Python syntax to modern idioms. pyupgrade --py311-plus src/ converts % formatting to f-strings, old-style super(), typing.List to list, typing.Optional to X|None. Idempotent. Pre-commit integration. MIT by Anthony Sottile. Many rules now available in ruff --fix. Best for post-version-drop modernization and enforcing contemporary Python style.
